questions.prompt: An increase in government spending (G) shifts which curve, and in which direction?
questions.explanation: Higher G raises autonomous spending, raising Y for any given r — that's a rightward shift of IS.

questions.prompt: Holding the IS curve fixed, an expansionary open-market operation will:
questions.explanation: Buying bonds raises M, shifts LM right, lowers r along the IS curve and raises Y.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are channels through which a higher interest rate reduces Y in IS-LM?
choices: Lower investment via I(r) = I0 − b·r
Lower money demand via k·Y − h·r
Multiplier compression of the autonomous shock
questions.explanation: Higher r lowers investment directly and crowds out private spending. Money demand effects are about market-clearing, not output, and the multiplier itself isn't an r-channel.

questions.prompt: With c = 0.6, t = 0.2, what is the Keynesian spending multiplier α = 1/(1 − c(1−t))? Round to two decimals.
Using the baseline parameters in the lesson chart (c = 0.6, t = 0.2, b = 20, k = 0.5, h = 10, P = 1, G = 100, M = 600, C0 + I0 = 200), compute the equilibrium interest rate r* to one decimal.
questions.explanation: Plug into Y* = (αhA + b·M/P)/(h + αbk) and r* = (k·Y* − M/P)/h. α ≈ 1.923, A = 300, giving Y* ≈ 423 and r* ≈ 6.4%.

furtherReading.title: How Much Can the Fed's Tightening Contract Global Economic Activity?
furtherReading.why: Real Fed tightening case showing how higher interest rates contract real activity and the goods market across countries — exactly the IS-LM transmission channel.
